hierarchyNote: Peak identity confirmed solid: name, coordinates (Wikipedia 48.9756,-120.1922; Mountain Project 48.97698,-120.19208 both match the given 48.97566,-120.192342 within survey tolerance), and parent (Pasayten Wilderness, Okanogan Range/North Cascades, Okanogan County) are corroborated across Wikipedia, Mountain Project, Mazamas, SummitPost search snippets, and USFS. One minor flag: elevation is reported inconsistently across sources — Wikipedia, Mountain Project, and Mazamas all cite 8,358 ft (older USGS Remmel Mountain quad figure), while ListsOfJohn.com (which uses newer LiDAR-derived data) lists 8,374 ft, matching the given DB value exactly. Both figures come from reputable sources; this looks like an old-topo-vs-LiDAR resurvey discrepancy rather than a wrong-peak issue, so the given 8,374 ft was retained rather than "corrected."
blurb: Amphitheater Mountain is a broad, multi-summited peak (South, West, and North sub-peaks in addition to the main summit) in the Pasayten Wilderness of the Okanogan Range, a sub-range of Washington's North Cascades, in Okanogan County just south of Cathedral Peak. It is a granite peak formed from accreted terranes roughly 50 million years ago and later sculpted by Pleistocene glaciation; its long north wall runs nearly three-quarters of a mile and holds several moderate alpine rock routes, while the west side is a straightforward talus/scree walk-up. The peak was first climbed via its easiest line in 1901 by Calkins and Smith.

routes.overview: The standard, non-technical line up Amphitheater Mountain: gain the peak's broad west-facing basin (the 'amphitheater') from Cathedral Pass/Upper Cathedral Lake and follow scree, grass, and boulder slopes to the summit plateau.
routes.beta: From Upper Cathedral Lake, follow a boot path southeast onto the west ridge/basin, then hike scree and grassy slopes northeasterly into a basin around 8,000 ft before a final walk to the summit. Alternatively, from the Cathedral Peak side a gully/col can be used to reach the same upper basin. The route is Class 2 throughout, with brief Class 3 sections depending on line choice; no rope or technical gear is used by most parties. Frequently combined with Cathedral Peak (Class 3, about one mile away) on the same trip.
routes.approach: Two common trailheads reach the base: (1) Andrews Creek Trailhead (Trail 504) — from Winthrop, take Hwy 20 to County Road 1213/West Chewuch Road, drive ~6 miles to Forest Road 51, continue ~22 miles to the trailhead at road's end (3,050 ft). Hike via Andrews Pass and the Boundary Trail to Upper Cathedral Lake (roughly 7 hours for a loaded backpacking pace). (2) Chewuch River Trail/Thirtymile Trailhead — a longer approach (about 20 miles and ~4,900 ft gain to Upper Cathedral Lake per trip reports), also joining near Cathedral Pass. Most parties backpack in and camp at or near Upper Cathedral Lake (~7,380 ft) before a summit day.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line back down through the west basin to Cathedral Pass/Upper Cathedral Lake.
routes.descentText: From the summit plateau, descend the same scree/grass slopes back into the west basin, then drop to Cathedral Pass and Upper Cathedral Lake to rejoin the approach trail. Parties combining with Cathedral Peak often traverse between the two via a connecting gully rather than returning all the way to the lake between summits.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to trailhead, backpack in (7+ hours) to camp at Upper Cathedral Lake. Day 2: Alpine-start summit day — climb Amphitheater Mountain (and optionally Cathedral Peak), return to camp. Day 3: Hike out.
routes.bail: This is a non-technical scramble route, so retreat is straightforward at any point — simply reverse course down the ascent slopes toward Cathedral Pass. In deteriorating weather (thunderstorms are common), descend into the basin toward Upper Cathedral Lake rather than lingering on exposed open slopes or the summit plateau.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before leaving camp and stick to it regardless of progress; afternoon thunderstorms build quickly over the exposed, open terrain of this route and there is little cover on the upper slopes or summit plateau.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September is the typical window; snow can linger in the approach basin/gullies into late June or early July, and by mid-July the Pasayten frequently enters seasonal fire restrictions with possible area closures. Many parties target early-to-mid July for snow-free travel before smoke/closure season, or wait until August for the most settled weather. By mid-to-late September nights turn cold and early snow becomes a risk at this elevation.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage anywhere in the Pasayten Wilderness backcountry (confirmed by multiple trip reports); carry a satellite communicator or personal locator beacon for emergencies.
routes.proNeeds: None required for the standard scramble; a helmet is recommended given loose rock on the upper slopes.

climate.typical: Dry, sunny summers with hot days and cool-to-cold nights; the Pasayten sits in the Cascades' rain shadow and is drier than the western slopes. Afternoon thunderstorms are common June-August, and wildfire smoke/closures often affect access from mid-July onward.
climate.spring: Still largely snowbound into June at this elevation; access roads may remain gated.
climate.summer: Prime climbing season, especially July-August; hot, dry days with afternoon thunderstorm risk and possible wildfire smoke/closures.
climate.fall: Cooling temperatures, generally stable weather and fewer bugs in early fall, with increasing risk of early snow by mid-to-late September.
climate.winter: Deep snowpack and closed access roads; no standard vehicle approach, winter travel only by ski/snowshoe.

routes.overview: A 4-pitch, 5.9 alpine trad line up the Ka'aba Buttress on Amphitheater Mountain's north wall, regarded as the area's best moderate rock route and requiring a long wilderness approach.
routes.beta: Four sustained pitches of crack and corner climbing (5.7, 5.8, the 5.9 'money pitch' with a twin handcrack, and a 5.8 finish) on generally solid, blocky rock, ending atop the Ka'aba Buttress above Upper Cathedral Lake.
routes.approach: Backpack in via Andrews Creek or Chewuch River trailheads to Upper Cathedral Lake (see West Route approach). From camp, walk about 15 minutes around the north side of Upper Cathedral Lake and scramble up to the base of the Ka'aba Buttress.
routes.descent: Walk east to the saddle of Amphitheater Mountain, then descend the trail south back toward Upper Cathedral Lake.
routes.descentText: From the top of the route, hike east to gain the saddle area of Amphitheater Mountain, then follow the descent trail/slopes south back down to Upper Cathedral Lake to rejoin the approach.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Backpack to Upper Cathedral Lake. Day 2: Climb Pilgrimage to Mecca (half-day route), descend to camp; optionally tag the summit or Cathedral Peak. Day 3: Hike out.
routes.bail: Rappel or downclimb from any of the four gear-anchor belays back to the base; the route is short enough that retreating from any pitch is straightforward with a standard rack.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-hour approach just to reach the base, start the climb early enough to be off the route and back at camp before afternoon thunderstorms typical of Pasayten summers.
routes.bestSeason: July through early September; the north-facing gully/moat at the base can hold firm snow into July, so early-season attempts should check current snow conditions.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in this part of the Pasayten Wilderness; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.detailedRack: Doubles from 0.3" to 3" (e.g. BD Camalots), one #4 cam, a full set of nuts, and small cams; build gear anchors/belays throughout.
routes.proNeeds: Standard trad rack with an emphasis on hand-to-fist-size cams for the crux twin-crack pitch; no fixed gear.

routes.overview: A 5-7 pitch, 5.5 alpine ridge climb up Amphitheater Mountain's blunt north ridge from Cathedral Pass, mixing scrambling with several short, steeper roped steps to the broad summit plateau.
routes.beta: From Cathedral Pass, scramble up to a point where roping up becomes worthwhile, then follow the blunt north ridge through several steps of up-to-5.5 climbing, with route-finding and loose blocks in places, topping out on Amphitheater's broad summit plateau.
routes.approach: Backpack to Upper Cathedral Lake/Cathedral Pass as for the West Route, then scramble to the base of the north ridge from the pass.
routes.descent: Either walk southwest around Amphitheater Mountain back to Upper Cathedral Lake, or descend via a small col southwest of the North Ridge down a 3rd-class gully on the north slope.
routes.descentText: From the summit plateau, either reverse toward Cathedral Pass and drop southwest around the mountain to Upper Cathedral Lake, or head to a small col just southwest of the North Ridge and downclimb a 3rd-class gully on the north slope back toward the approach.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Backpack to Upper Cathedral Lake/camp near Cathedral Pass. Day 2: Climb the North Ridge, descend, return to camp. Day 3: Hike out.
routes.bail: The route can be downclimbed or rappelled from gear anchors on most of the steps below the crux sections; below the ridge's roped sections, retreat to Cathedral Pass is straightforward scrambling.
routes.turnaround: Loose rock and route-finding make this a poor route to be caught on in deteriorating weather; turn around with ample margin if storms build.
routes.bestSeason: July through September, once the ridge is free of lingering snow.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in this part of the Pasayten Wilderness.
routes.detailedRack: A light rack of nuts and long slings is sufficient given the moderate grade and mostly natural gear placements/horns along the ridge.
routes.proNeeds: Light trad rack (nuts + slings); this is more of an alpine scramble-with-pitches than a sustained crack climb.

routes.overview: A 4-pitch, 5.10b alpine trad route up the left side of Amphitheater's largest buttress above Upper Cathedral Lake, starting with a striking hand crack out of a chimney.
routes.beta: Begin in a chimney (optional belay at its top), then climb an excellent hand crack (the route's hardest and best pitch), traverse right along a dihedral, and finish on non-sustained 5.7-5.9 terrain to the top, with a possible 5th pitch depending on the exact line chosen.

routes.descent: Hike east along the crest to a small col southwest of the North Ridge, then descend a 3rd-class gully down the north slope.
routes.descentText: From the top of the buttress, walk east along the ridge crest to the small col located southwest of the North Ridge, then downclimb the 3rd-class gully on the north slope back toward the approach trail.

routes.overview: A hard, 5-pitch 5.11c alpine trad testpiece up the Middle Finger Buttress, joining the Middle Finger Buttress-Left route near the top before a summit scramble.
routes.beta: Pitch 1 climbs a rightward-trending ramp/flake (5.10-ish); pitch 2 is the crux, a ringlock crack, stem corner, and leftward traverse to a belay beneath roofs (5.11c); pitch 3 continues through underclings and jams beneath two roofs into a chimney (5.11b); pitches 4-5 follow hand cracks and blocky terrain trending right, joining the Middle Finger Buttress-Left line on a rib before a scramble to the top.
routes.approach: Backpack to Upper Cathedral Lake as for the West Route; the face is directly above the lake on the Middle Finger Buttress.
routes.descent: As for Middle Finger Buttress routes: hike east along the ridge crest to the small col southwest of the North Ridge, then descend the 3rd-class gully on the north slope.

hierarchyNote: Peak identity and location check out: Big Snagtooth (48°32'05"N 120°35'22"W, 8,374-8,379 ft per Wikipedia/ListsOfJohn/Country Highpoints LiDAR resurvey, closely matching the given 48.534263,-120.588409 / 8,379 ft) is a real, well-documented North Cascades summit near Washington Pass, Okanogan County WA, first climbed by Fred Beckey, Jerry O'Neil, and Charles Welsh on Sept 29, 1946. One nuance: multiple sources (Wikipedia's Snagtooth Ridge article, SummitPost) identify Big Snagtooth specifically as the high point of "Snagtooth Ridge," a distinct ridge that sits between Silver Star Mountain and Kangaroo Ridge (not physically part of Kangaroo Ridge itself). The given parent area "Washington Pass (Kangaroo Ridge)" appears to be used as a broader regional grouping label for the Washington Pass corridor rather than a literal statement that Big Snagtooth sits on Kangaroo Ridge — worth a look if the catalog distinguishes Snagtooth Ridge as its own sub-area elsewhere. Not blocking; proceeding with research under the given id.
blurb: Big Snagtooth (8,374-8,379 ft) is the highest point on Snagtooth Ridge, a jagged granitic ridge in the North Cascades that runs between Silver Star Mountain and Kangaroo Ridge near Washington Pass in Okanogan County, Washington. One of the Washington "Bulgers" (state top-100 peaks by elevation), it is a rugged, seldom-climbed summit reached via a long, brushy off-trail approach up the Willow Creek drainage, capped by a short but airy, largely unprotectable summit block. It was first climbed on September 29, 1946 by Fred Beckey, Jerry O'Neil, and Charles Welsh.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Jerry O'Neil, and Charles Welsh, September 29, 1946 — this is documented as the peak's overall first ascent; sources do not explicitly confirm the 1946 party followed this exact line, but the West Ridge is the standard/easiest way to the summit block and is treated by SummitPost/Mountaineers as the natural original line.
routes.rappels: 2 short rappels off the summit block (a 30m rope is sufficient for both)
routes.season: Jun-Sep (bounded by the seasonal SR-20 road closure; a lingering spring snowpack in the upper basin, into June, gives firmer, faster travel than the loose late-summer scree)
routes.face: West/southwest ridge above the Willow Creek basin
routes.overview: The West Ridge is Big Snagtooth's standard route: a long, mostly non-technical off-trail approach and class 2 ridge walk that ends with two short, exposed technical steps — a class 4/low-5th chimney followed by an unprotectable 5.7-5.8 boulder/friction move — onto the small, airy true summit.
routes.beta: From a roadside pullout on SR-20 east of Washington Pass, descend/cross Early Winters Creek and climb steeply up the Willow Creek drainage (brushy, staying on the north side) to about 5,000 ft where the grade eases. Continue up open basin terrain to roughly 7,000 ft, then cross a loose scree or snow slope (~400 ft) to gain the west ridge at a saddle near 8,250 ft. Follow the class 2 ridge crest to the base of the summit block, climb a short class 4/low-5th chimney/crack (about 50 ft) to a stance below the final slab, then make an unprotected 5.7-5.8 boulder move (often boosted via a shoulder stand) onto the roughly 20-ft summit friction slab and the true summit.
routes.approach: Park in a highway pullout on SR-20 about 3.5 miles east of the Washington Pass summit (roughly 4,320 ft elevation). Descend to and cross Early Winters Creek, then head up into the Willow Creek valley, staying on the north side of the creek. The first ~3/4 mile up the valley is steep and can be very brushy in summer. Around 5,000 ft the slope eases; continue up loose/open terrain into the upper basin near 7,000 ft, then traverse a loose scree or snow slope for about 400 ft to reach the saddle on the west ridge (~8,250 ft).
routes.descent: Reverse the route: friction-downclimb and rappel (2 short raps, 30m rope) off the summit block, then descend the class 2 west ridge and scree/basin back into the Willow Creek drainage to the highway.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb about 10 ft to a rappel anchor and rappel the short friction slab, then downclimb/scramble back to the top of the lower chimney and rappel (existing webbing/anchors reported) to the ridge below. From there, descend the class 2 west ridge back to the upper basin, retrace the scree/snow traverse, and descend the Willow Creek drainage (loose, brushy) back to the SR-20 pullout. A 30m rope is sufficient for both rappels.
routes.itinerary: Typically climbed as a single long day car-to-car; trip reports describe full days with parties returning close to sunset given the brushy approach, loose scree, and slow travel. A 2-day/overnight option with a basin bivy is sometimes used, especially when combined with an adjacent objective (e.g., a Silver Star–Big Snagtooth traverse, reported at 12-18 hours).
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward below the summit block: the technical crux (chimney + slab) can simply be downclimbed/reversed by not committing to the final unprotected move, and the rest of the route is class 2 ridge and scree/basin travel back to Willow Creek and the highway.
routes.turnaround: Because the final slab move is essentially unprotected and the descent through loose scree and brush is slow, plan a turnaround time that leaves ample daylight margin — parties who linger near the summit have reported finishing the descent close to dark even on long summer days.
routes.bestSeason: Spring into early summer (roughly once SR-20 opens for the season through June) is often cited as the best window, since residual snow in the upper basin firms up the loose ground and eases travel; by mid-to-late summer the route becomes a loose scree ('kitty litter') and talus slog with more exposed rock. The peak is effectively inaccessible in winter, since SR-20 is gated and closed for the season (typically closed by early December, reopening in spring on a variable date — as late as mid/late June in years with major storm/rockslide damage, as in 2026).
routes.detailedRack: A very light alpine rack is standard: 2 small cams and 2 medium cams cover the short protectable section below the final slab; a single 30m rope is enough for the two rappels off the summit block.
routes.proNeeds: The crux 5.7-5.8 slab/boulder move on the true summit is essentially unprotectable and is often surmounted with a shoulder stand or boost rather than free-climbed on gear. The short class 4/low-5th chimney just below it is the only real placement opportunity and typically takes 2-3 small-to-medium cams.
gear: light rack (2 small cams, 2 medium cams)
whatToBring: helmet for loose rock
gaiters for scree travel
sturdy approach shoes/boots with good friction for the summit slab
extra water — no reliable sources above Willow Creek
sun protection for the exposed upper ridge
proTips: Go in late spring/early summer while snow still covers the upper basin — it firms up the ground and is faster and more secure than the loose scree found later in summer.
Stay on the north side of Willow Creek on the lower approach to minimize brush-fighting.
Many parties boost the leader onto the crux summit slab with a shoulder stand rather than attempting to free-climb the unprotected 5.7-5.8 move.
watchOut: The final summit move is on an unprotectable friction slab — a fall here would be serious.
The upper-mountain scree is unusually loose ('kitty litter over sand'), even by North Cascades standards.
There are no reliable water sources once you leave Willow Creek.
knownHazards: Loose rock and unstable talus/scree throughout the upper approach and ridge
Unprotected 5.7-5.8 slab/boulder climbing on the summit block
Dense brush in the lower Willow Creek approach, especially by mid-summer
Full sun/weather exposure on the open ridge crest with no shade or bail shelter

pitchDetail.notes: West ridge crest from the ~8,250 ft saddle to the base of the summit block; scrambling, no rope typically needed.
Short chimney/crack (~50 ft) to a stance below the final slab; the main protectable section, usually 2-3 small-medium cams.
~20 ft unprotectable friction/boulder move onto the true summit, commonly aided by a shoulder stand; airy but low exposure (~5 ft) if roped from below.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR-20)
road.seasonalGate: Gated each winter between the Ross Dam trailhead (MP 134) and the Silver Star gate (MP 171), which brackets the Washington Pass corridor; typically closed by early December and reopened in spring on a variable date once avalanche and rockslide hazards are cleared (as late as mid/late June in years with major winter damage, e.g. 2026).
road.driveNote: Roadside pullout on SR-20 about 3.5 miles east of the Washington Pass summit, at roughly 4,320 ft elevation; there is no formal trailhead lot — this is an off-trail approach.

climate.typical: Continental-influenced east-slope Cascades climate — drier and less snow-laden than west-side approaches, but the upper basin still holds a spring snowpack into early summer.
climate.spring: Snow typically still covers the upper basin and approach gullies into June, giving firmer travel but requiring standard snow travel judgment.
climate.summer: Snow melts out, exposing loose scree and talus ('kitty litter'); hot, sun-exposed climbing on the open ridge with no shade.
climate.fall: Possible early-season snow; the SR-20 access road closes for the winter by early December.
climate.winter: SR-20 is gated and closed; the peak is essentially inaccessible without a long snowmobile/ski approach and is rarely attempted.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District
access.permit: None required for this off-trail scramble/climb
access.passRequired: None required (no Northwest Forest Pass or permit noted for this trailhead)
access.closures: No route-specific closures found beyond the seasonal SR-20 winter road closure that gates all access to the trailhead.

timing.recommendedStart: An early/pre-dawn start is advisable given the long, loose approach and the exposed, unprotected summit block — several trip reports describe full days finishing close to sunset.

waypoints.name: SR-20 Pullout / Willow Creek approach start

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Okanogan County Sheriff's Office / 911 Dispatch Center — non-emergency (509) 422-7232
emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District, 24 West Chewuch Rd, Winthrop, WA — (509) 996-4000
emergency.nearestHospital: Three Rivers Hospital, 507 Hospital Way, Brewster, WA — (509) 689-2517 (Mid-Valley Hospital in Omak, (509) 826-1760, is a comparable alternative distance away)
emergency.notes: Remote off-trail approach through a brushy, steep drainage with likely poor or no cell coverage; carry a satellite communicator and be prepared for self-rescue given the low traffic on this route.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation (8,379 ft) matches the modern LiDAR/GPS-based figure from ListsOfJohn; note that many still-current route pages (Mountaineers, WTA, older guidebook-derived sources) cite the legacy pre-LiDAR quad-surveyed elevation of 8,330 ft for the summit — the quad missed the true highest contour on this sharp peak. Wikipedia's Snagtooth Ridge article gives 8,374 ft from a slightly different resurvey rounding. All three cluster within ~50 ft of each other and the given value; no change made to the peak record, flagged here for context on the route-stat sources used (gainFt/highPointFt above follow Mountaineers' stats page, which itself still uses the older 8,330 ft summit figure for its gain calculation).

blurb: Elephant Butte is a 7,384-foot summit in the Picket Range within North Cascades National Park's Stephen Mather Wilderness, built of orthogneiss and rising steeply above McMillan Creek to the north (about 5,200 ft of relief in roughly one mile). It sits near the center of the range close to the Southern Pickets and requires no technical climbing to reach its summit, but is rarely visited as a stand-alone objective — most parties encounter it only while traversing between Sourdough/Stetattle Ridge and the Southern Pickets, and it is estimated to see a dozen or fewer visits per year.

objHaz: Short overhung 5th-class step directly over the summit (not advisable with an overnight pack)
Steep, brushy, mossy-cliff bypass terrain with real route-finding difficulty
Class 3 rock steps/mantle moves along the ridge
Early- and late-season snowfields on the ridge crest
Sourdough Creek crossing can be hazardous in spring (thin snow bridge over high water)
Dense brush and heavy mosquito pressure in summer
routes.season: Jul-Sep (best); ridge and bypass terrain typically not clear until mid-summer
routes.face: Approached from the west via Sourdough/Stetattle Ridge; the peak's steep north face rises directly above McMillan Creek
routes.overview: A long, mostly non-technical ridge approach and scramble to Elephant Butte via the Sourdough Mountain Trail and the open Sourdough/Stetattle Ridge crest. The crux is not technical climbing but a short, exposed, brush-and-cliff obstacle immediately below the summit that most parties bypass rather than climb directly.
routes.beta: From the Diablo trailhead, the maintained Sourdough Mountain Trail is followed for roughly 4-5 miles and about 4,300-4,900 ft of gain before leaving the trail on an unmarked climber's path to reach the open crest of Sourdough/Stetattle Ridge. From there it's an extended, largely trail-less ridge walk (with a short class 3 step near a saddle) toward Elephant Butte. The peak itself presents an obstacle: a short overhung 5th-class move goes more or less directly over it (not recommended with an overnight pack), while most parties instead drop well off the ridge and traverse below/around the peak through brush and mossy micro-cliffs before regaining the ridge crest beyond it. Most climbers do this as part of a multi-day traverse into or out of the Southern Pickets rather than as a dedicated objective, though a very long single-day round trip from Diablo is feasible for fit, fast parties.
routes.approach: Drive State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway) to milepost 126 and turn off at the sign for the village of Diablo, a Seattle City Light company town. Park near the river across from the domed swimming pool; the Sourdough Mountain Trail begins immediately behind the pool building (roughly 890-900 ft). Follow the trail as it switchbacks steeply through forest, crossing Sourdough Creek around mile 4 (use caution in spring — the crossing can be only a thin snow bridge over high water). At an unmarked junction around 4,300-4,400 ft, leave the maintained trail and head up onto the open crest of Sourdough/Stetattle Ridge (roughly 6,500 ft) rather than continuing to the Sourdough Mountain Lookout. From the ridge crest, continue along the mostly open, trail-less ridge toward Elephant Butte.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: back along Sourdough/Stetattle Ridge to the unmarked trail junction, then down the Sourdough Mountain Trail to Diablo.
routes.descentText: From the summit, retrace the approach: regain the ridge crest above the Elephant Butte bypass terrain, then follow the open ridge back toward the Sourdough Mountain Trail junction (around 6,500 ft, then the junction at 4,300-4,400 ft). Drop onto the maintained trail and switchback down through forest to the trailhead at Diablo. Parties using this as a through-route to the Southern Pickets instead continue along the ridge system toward Pioneer Ridge/McMillan Creek and the Pickets rather than returning to Diablo.
routes.itinerary: As a dedicated objective: Day 1 — drive to Diablo, hike the Sourdough Mountain Trail and ridge to a camp near Sourdough/Stetattle Ridge; Day 2 — continue the ridge traverse to Elephant Butte's summit and return. A fit, light party can also do the full round trip from Diablo in one very long day. As an approach leg into the Southern Pickets, most parties budget two to three days from Diablo to reach Elephant Butte with full overnight packs before continuing into the range.
routes.bail: The Sourdough Mountain Trail provides a clear, maintained retreat once regained; from anywhere on the open ridge, the safest bail is to retrace the route to the unmarked trail junction (~4,300-4,400 ft) and descend the trail to Diablo rather than attempting to shortcut off the ridge through unknown terrain.
routes.turnaround: Because the return crosses exposed, trail-less ridge terrain and the brushy/cliffy bypass around Elephant Butte, set a firm turnaround time that leaves enough daylight to get back across that terrain and down the steep trail before dark, with a margin for slower travel when fatigued.
routes.bestSeason: Most ascents are made from July through September, once the ridge crest and the bypass terrain around Elephant Butte have largely melted out; documented ascents span early September, mid/late June (as part of a full Pickets traverse), and July. Snow can linger on the ridge into early summer, and the exposed ridge crest is not a realistic winter/spring objective.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed or placed protection is used on this route — it is off-trail hiking/scrambling rather than roped climbing. An ice axe is useful for lingering early-season snow patches on the ridge; some parties carry light rope/webbing for confidence on the class 3 step, though it is not standard practice.
gear: Sturdy boots with good ankle support
Topo map, compass, and/or GPS (extensive off-trail route-finding)
whatToBring: Overnight backpacking/camping gear if splitting the approach over 2-3 days
Water treatment (limited reliable water sources high on the ridge)
Sun protection for long exposed ridge walking
Extra food and time buffer — this route commonly takes longer than expected
proTips: Get beta on the brush/mossy-cliff bypass line around Elephant Butte before committing — parties with prior knowledge move through it far faster than those finding it cold
Watch for the unmarked climber's path leaving the maintained Sourdough Mountain Trail around 4,300-4,400 ft; it is easy to walk past
Most parties treat Elephant Butte as a waypoint on a longer Pickets traverse rather than a stand-alone dayhike objective, and plan food/time accordingly
watchOut: The direct line over Elephant Butte involves a short overhung 5th-class move — not advisable with an overnight pack
The bypass around the peak is brushy, mossy, and cliffy with genuine route-finding difficulty; some parties report getting off-route into 'horror-story' cliffy micro-terrain
Sourdough Creek (~mile 4 on the trail) can be hazardous in spring when only a thin snow bridge spans high water
knownHazards: Loose, exposed class 3 rock on the ridge and near Elephant Butte
Steep brushy/mossy terrain with poor footing on the bypass around the peak
Early- and late-season snowfields on the ridge crest
Very long, remote approach far from any maintained trail or quick rescue access

pitchDetail.notes: Sourdough Mountain Trail from Diablo to the unmarked ridge junction, ~4,300-4,400 ft, roughly 4-5 miles
Leave the trail, ascend open slopes to the crest of Sourdough/Stetattle Ridge (~6,500 ft)
Open ridge walking toward Elephant Butte; one class 3 step near a saddle (two high-step mantle moves)
Drop off the ridge and traverse brushy, mossy micro-cliff terrain around Elephant Butte, then regain the ridge to the summit

road.name: North Cascades Highway (State Route 20)
road.status: Paved; the west-side section to Diablo/Newhalem (around milepost 120-126) typically stays open year-round even when the high pass is closed
road.seasonalGate: The high-elevation section of SR-20 east of Diablo (through Washington Pass) closes seasonally, roughly mid-November to mid/late-April, for avalanche danger; partial-season closure gates are at Newhalem (MP 120) and Early Winters (MP 178)
road.driveNote: Take SR-20 to milepost 126 and turn off at the sign for the village of Diablo (a Seattle City Light company town); park near the river across from the domed swimming pool.

climate.typical: Wet, maritime North Cascades climate with snow lingering into summer on shaded, north-facing terrain
climate.spring: Heavy snowpack persists on the ridge crest into June
climate.summer: Primary climbing season (July-September), though thunderstorms and low cloud off Diablo/Gorge Lake are common
climate.fall: Snow can return to the high ridge by late September/October
climate.winter: Deep, avalanche-prone snowpack; SR-20's high pass is closed and this route is not a realistic winter objective

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex / Stephen Mather Wilderness (National Park Service)
access.fees: Backcountry/wilderness camping permits cost $10 per person plus a $6 reservation fee (youth 15 and under free) when reserved through Recreation.gov; a portion of sites are held for walk-up permits
access.permit: A wilderness/backcountry permit is required for overnight camping; obtain in advance via Recreation.gov or as a walk-up permit at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount
access.closures: SR-20 closes seasonally at higher elevations east of Diablo for avalanche danger, but the Diablo trailhead itself is on the year-round-open west side of the highway

emergency.county: Whatcom County, WA (Diablo trailhead); North Cascades National Park spans Whatcom, Skagit, and Chelan counties
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office; backcountry search-and-rescue within the park is coordinated primarily through North Cascades National Park dispatch/rangers
emergency.rangerStation: Wilderness Information Center, 7280 Ranger Station Road, Marblemount, WA - (360) 854-7245 / noca_wilderness@nps.gov
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, 2000 Hospital Dr, Sedro-Woolley, WA - roughly 90 minutes' drive from the Diablo/SR-20 corridor
emergency.notes: Remote, mostly off-trail terrain with no reliable cell coverage; NPS notes the closest advanced life-support ambulance is based in Concrete, about an hour away, so self-rescue/evacuation planning matters.

blurb: Castle Peak, known to climbers simply as "The Castle," is a distinctive, block-shaped summit near the center of the Tatoosh Range in Mount Rainier National Park, immediately east of Pinnacle Peak. Like the rest of the range, its lower flanks are carved from the ~14-26 million-year-old Tatoosh granodiorite pluton, capped by steeper red-to-dark-gray volcanic rock near the top. It is one of the most technical of the range's popular scrambles — even the easiest line to the true (northernmost) summit requires a short, exposed pitch of low 5th-class rock — and it is a favorite quick objective and ski-mountaineering target thanks to its short approach from Reflection Lakes or Narada Falls.

routes.overview: Castle Peak's easiest and by far most-traveled line: a class 2-3 approach and traverse around Pinnacle Peak's south flank to the Castle-Pinnacle col, followed by a short, well-protected easy-5th-class crack-and-chimney pitch up the southeast/east flank to the exposed summit crest.
routes.beta: From the Pinnacle Saddle Trailhead at Reflection Lakes, hike the Pinnacle Peak Trail to the ~5,920-ft saddle south of Pinnacle Peak, then leave the trail and contour east across Pinnacle's south-face scree/talus (roughly 6,000-6,200 ft) to the Castle-Pinnacle col. From the col, scramble onto Castle's south rib, make a short class-3 traverse onto the east flank, and follow the obvious crack-chimney system (easy 5th class; some parties call this the East Face Chimney Route) up and right to a notch beside a natural rock horn. A brief, exposed class 3-4 scramble along the summit crest leads to the true (northernmost) summit block. Many parties fix a handline or belay the chimney pitch and always rappel or downclimb it on the return.
routes.approach: Park at the Reflection Lakes pullouts on Stevens Canyon Road (or, when the road is snowed in, at the year-round Narada Falls lot). Take the signed Pinnacle Peak Trail from the south side of the road; it climbs steadily through forest and subalpine meadow for about 1.3 miles and 1,050 ft of gain to the 5,920-ft saddle just south of Pinnacle Peak. From the saddle, instead of continuing up Pinnacle, traverse climber's-left (east) across Pinnacle's south-facing scree/boulder field, staying around 6,000-6,200 ft, to the col between Pinnacle and Castle — roughly another half mile of cross-country travel to the base of Castle's south/southeast side.
routes.descent: Reverse the route: downclimb or rappel the chimney pitch from the notch below the summit block, then retrace the traverse across Pinnacle's south face to the saddle and descend the Pinnacle Peak Trail to Reflection Lakes.
routes.descentText: From the true summit, downclimb the short, exposed crest back to the notch above the crack-chimney pitch. Most parties rappel from a sturdy natural rock horn/block near the notch back down to the base of the technical section (carry your own webbing/rap rings rather than relying on tat left by other groups). From there, downclimb the class-3 section back onto the south rib, retrace the contouring traverse across Pinnacle's scree slope to the Pinnacle-Castle col and back to the main saddle, then descend the Pinnacle Peak Trail to Reflection Lakes. In winter and spring the same line is often skied or glissaded from the col.
routes.itinerary: A single long day from the Puget Sound area: drive to Reflection Lakes (roughly 2.5-3 hours from Seattle), scramble/climb to the summit (about 2-3 hours including the traverse and technical pitch), spend 30-60 minutes on top, and descend in 1.5-2.5 hours — a full round trip of roughly 4-6 hours car-to-car. Often combined with Pinnacle Peak and/or Plummer Peak in the same outing since the approach trail is shared.
routes.bail: The technical crux is a single, short pitch close to the col, so retreat is straightforward at any point below the summit block: downclimb or rappel back to the notch and reverse the approach traverse. There is no separate established bail line partway up the chimney other than reversing it.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting the technical pitch — afternoon thunderstorms build quickly over the Tatoosh in summer, and wet or snow-covered rock on the southeast face is significantly harder and more dangerous to downclimb or rappel. If the crux pitch is wet, icy, or the party is moving slowly, turn back rather than push for the summit.
routes.bestSeason: Prime scrambling season is roughly late June/July through September, once Stevens Canyon Road is open and the route has melted out; the trail and technical pitch are typically fully clear by mid-to-late summer. The peak is also a well-known ski-mountaineering objective in winter and spring, approached instead from the year-round Narada Falls parking lot, with the southeast-face rock pitch downclimbed, rappelled, or bypassed depending on snow coverage.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable throughout the Tatoosh backcountry; a signal booster serves the Paradise visitor-center area but reception cannot be relied on at Reflection Lakes, on the Pinnacle Peak Trail, or on Castle itself — carry a satellite communicator or PLB for emergencies.
routes.rappels: One rappel (roughly a rope-length or less) from a natural rock horn/block near the summit notch back down the crack-chimney pitch.
objHaz: wet/icy rock in the chimney early season
routes.season: Jun-Sep (also a popular winter/spring ski approach from Narada Falls)
routes.detailedRack: A light rack suffices: a few small-to-medium cams (finger to hand size, e.g. a #2 Camalot for the crux crack) and a couple of nuts/slings; many experienced parties solo or simul-climb the short technical section using just a handline. Bring 20-30 ft of extra webbing/rap rings to back up or replace tat on the rappel horn.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is sparse on the chimney/crack pitch — cams from small to about hand-size protect the crux crack, but much of the climbing is on low-angle, featured rock that many parties solo. A single-rope rappel (plus spare webbing for the anchor) handles the descent.
gear: Rope (30m is sufficient for the single rappel)
Small rock rack (cams to about #2 Camalot, a few nuts)
Ice axe and traction devices (early season or snow-covered approach)
whatToBring: Sturdy scrambling boots or approach shoes
Map/compass or GPS (the Pinnacle traverse is not an obvious trail)
Sun protection (fully exposed terrain)
Extra layers
Satellite communicator/PLB
proTips: Combine Castle with Pinnacle Peak and/or Plummer Peak for a full Tatoosh day — the approach trail and traverse are shared.
The crack-chimney pitch is short enough that a single 30m rope is plenty for the rappel.
Go early or on a weekday in summer — this is one of the most popular scrambles in the park and the small summit block gets crowded.
watchOut: Loose rock is common on the approach traverse and in the chimney — helmets are recommended for the whole party, not just the leader.
The rock can stay wet or icy into early summer in shaded sections of the chimney, making the 'easy 5th class' pitch feel much harder than the grade suggests.
Trip reports and photos show climbers stopping below the true (northernmost) summit block — confirm you're on the correct high point.
knownHazards: Exposed, largely unprotected class 3-4 terrain along the summit ridge crest.
A short but real 5th-class pitch with sparse natural protection.
Loose volcanic rock in places, especially off-route or on the approach scree traverse.
Rapid, thunderstorm-prone afternoon weather typical of the Cascades in summer.

pitchDetail.notes: Pinnacle Peak Trail from Reflection Lakes to the 5,920-ft saddle: 1.3 mi, ~1,050 ft gain.
Leave the trail and contour east across Pinnacle's south-face scree/talus (~6,000-6,200 ft) to the Castle-Pinnacle col.
Crack-chimney system on the SE/E flank to a notch beside a natural rock horn; sparse protection, a #2 Camalot covers the crux.
Short, airy crest traverse from the notch to the true (northernmost) summit block.

road.name: Stevens Canyon Road (from Longmire/Paradise) to Reflection Lakes
road.seasonalGate: Gated and closed by snow roughly mid-October through late May/early June, depending on snowpack; also subject to intermittent summer closures for road-rehab construction — check NPS road status before driving.
road.driveNote: From the Nisqually (SW) entrance, drive via Longmire and Paradise to the Stevens Canyon Road junction, then east about 1.5 miles to the Reflection Lakes pullouts; the Pinnacle Peak/Castle trailhead is on the south side of the road, directly across from the lakes. In winter, park instead at the year-round Narada Falls lot and approach on skis/snowshoes.

climate.forecastZone: NWS Seattle (SEW) Mount Rainier Recreational Forecast, Paradise-area zone
climate.typical: Marine west-coast mountain climate: wet, snowy winters and drier, sunnier summers, with rapid weather changes and frequent afternoon convective buildups common to the southern Washington Cascades.
climate.spring: Still largely snow-covered; ski/snowshoe approach from Narada Falls is typical, with variable avalanche and wet-slide hazard.
climate.summer: Warmest and driest window (Jun-Sep); the standard scramble route is normally fully melted out by mid-to-late summer, though afternoon thunderstorms are common.
climate.fall: Cooling and wetter, with the first snows and Stevens Canyon Road's seasonal closure typically arriving by mid-October.
climate.winter: Heavy snowfall and avalanche hazard; Stevens Canyon Road is gated, so access is via Narada Falls on skis or snowshoes.

access.landManager: National Park Service — Mount Rainier National Park
access.fees: $30 per private vehicle (7-day pass) or $15 per person on foot/bicycle; America the Beautiful and other NPS annual passes are accepted.
access.permit: No special climbing permit is required for Castle Peak — Mount Rainier's mandatory climbing-registration fee/permit applies only above roughly 10,000 ft or to glacier travel, neither of which applies in the Tatoosh Range. A backcountry wilderness permit is only needed for overnight camping, not a day climb.
access.passRequired: Standard park entrance pass/fee (see fees) — no separate climbing pass needed.
access.closures: Stevens Canyon Road (the Reflection Lakes access) is closed seasonally by snow, typically mid-October to late May/early June; watch for additional summer construction closures. The Butter Creek Research Natural Area nearby is closed to public entry except under a scientific research permit.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning start, both to beat crowds on this popular scramble and to be off the exposed summit block before typical Cascade afternoon thunderstorms build.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Lewis County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: (360) 748-9286 (dial 911 for active emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: Longmire Wilderness Information Center / Ranger Station: (360) 569-6650; MORA park headquarters: (360) 569-2211
emergency.nearestHospital: Arbor Health — Morton Hospital, Morton/Packwood, WA (nearest hospital to the south-side Paradise/Reflection Lakes access; Providence Centralia Hospital in Centralia, WA is a larger regional facility farther away)
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable throughout the area; report emergencies to park rangers at Longmire/Paradise or via 911 when signal allows, or carry a satellite communicator/PLB.

routes.corrections: The peak's given elevation (6,469 ft) does not match the best-corroborated figure. Wikipedia (citing the official USGS/GNIS feature record, GNIS ID 1533633), the Mazamas route page, and an independent trip report all independently give 6,440 ft (1,963 m); other sources vary widely (SummitPost/older pages ~6,640 ft, a Mountain Project crag-area page 6,393 ft, one blog 6,460 ft) but 6,440 ft is used here as highPointFt since it is corroborated by 2+ independent reputable sources including the authoritative USGS/GNIS record.

routes.overview: A three-star, low-fifth-class trad line documented in the small rock-climbing area on The Castle, distinct from (and reportedly higher quality than) the standard scramble/chimney route to the summit.
routes.beta: Listed on Mountain Project as a 5.7 trad route in The Castle's climbing area alongside the easy-5th-class Southeast Face; no detailed pitch-by-pitch description, approach specifics, or first-ascent history were found in available sources. Likely approached the same way as the Southeast Face — via the Pinnacle Peak Trail from Reflection Lakes and a traverse across Pinnacle's south-face scree to the Castle-Pinnacle area.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time — afternoon thunderstorms build quickly over the Tatoosh in summer, and this range offers no easy bail once committed to technical rock.
routes.bestSeason: Likely climbable in the same window as the rest of the peak's rock routes, roughly late June/July through September once Stevens Canyon Road is open and the rock has dried out.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable throughout the Tatoosh backcountry; carry a satellite communicator or PLB for emergencies.

hierarchyNote: Confirmed as a real, well-documented peak (Wenatchee Ridge, North Cascades, Chelan County; elevation 7,447-7,448 ft per Wikipedia/listsofjohn, matching given data within normal survey variance; given coordinates 48.007925,-121.098305 are within roughly 300m of Wikipedia's 48.0081,-121.0978 and Natural Atlas's 48.0063,-121.1015 -- consistent with the named peak). The parent area "Glacier Peak Wilderness / Mountain Loop Hwy" is geographically correct for the standard approach: the North Fork Sauk Trailhead used by virtually all trip reports for this peak is reached via Forest Road 49 off the Mountain Loop Highway from Darrington, inside Glacier Peak Wilderness (Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF). The summit itself sits on the Chelan County / Okanogan-Wenatchee NF side of the ridge crest per Wikipedia, but the standard climbing approach is entirely from the Mountain Loop Hwy (Snohomish County) side, so the given parent-area label matches how climbers actually reach it.
blurb: Indian Head Peak (7,447-7,448 ft) is the second-highest point on Wenatchee Ridge in the Glacier Peak Wilderness of the North Cascades, composed of Late Cretaceous-age schist shaped by past glaciation into the U-shaped valleys below it. Surveyor Albert H. Sylvester named it for its resemblance, seen from the south near Kodak Peak, to an Indian headdress. The peak was reportedly first climbed on July 2, 1870 by railroad survey party members D.C. Linsley and John A. Tennant; today it is a remote, seldom-crowded off-trail scramble reached by a long backpack to Indian Pass on the Pacific Crest Trail.

routes.overview: The standard non-technical line: a long backpack approach via White Pass to Indian Pass on the PCT, then an off-trail scramble up the peak's south/southwest slopes onto its west ridge, finishing with a short exposed move onto the southeast face to reach the true (east) summit.
routes.beta: From a backcountry camp near Indian Pass (5,000 ft) on the Pacific Crest Trail, climbers leave the trail and head up bushy hillside on the peak's southwest/south slopes; the terrain opens up above the brush into Class 2 talus and boulder slopes. The line trends up onto the west ridge and follows it toward the summit block. The ridge holds twin summits close in height -- most parties stop at the west (false) summit where a register sits, but trip reports and elevation checks indicate the east summit is 15-40 feet higher and has better Class 3 scrambling; reaching it requires a short, loose, exposed corkscrew move around onto the peak's southeast face near the top.
routes.approach: Drive the Mountain Loop Highway from Darrington (WA-530) roughly 17-19.7 miles, then turn onto Forest Road 49 (Sloan Creek Road) and continue about 6.6-6.7 miles to the North Fork Sauk Trailhead (2,070 ft, vault toilet, no potable water). Hike the North Fork Sauk Trail #649 through old-growth forest, passing Red Creek camp (3.5 mi) and Mackinaw Shelter (5 mi), then climbing roughly 3,000 ft over the final miles to the Pacific Crest Trail junction (~8.2 mi from the trailhead). Follow the PCT north about 0.7 mi to White Pass, then continue about 2 more miles on the PCT to Indian Pass (~4,962-5,000 ft), the low point/junction with the Indian Creek Trail and the jumping-off point for the peak. Total one-way approach to Indian Pass is roughly 10.9-11 miles.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: downclimb off the southeast face back onto the west ridge, retrace the southwest slopes back to Indian Pass, then return via White Pass and the North Fork Sauk Trail to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the true (east) summit, carefully reverse the short exposed corkscrew move back onto the west ridge/false summit area, then descend the same Class 2 talus and boulder slopes on the south/southwest face used on the ascent, watching for loose rock. Regain the bushy hillside below and return to a camp near Indian Pass. From there, retrace the PCT south to White Pass and drop back onto the North Fork Sauk Trail for the long hike out to the trailhead. No rappels are needed on this route; it is a walk-down of the ascent line.
routes.itinerary: Typically done as a 2-day (3-day for a more relaxed pace) backpack: Day 1 hike the North Fork Sauk Trail from the trailhead to a camp near White Pass or Indian Pass (roughly 11 miles, ~4,000+ ft gain); Day 2 climb Indian Head Peak round-trip from camp (off-trail scramble, several hours) and either hike out or camp again; optional Day 3 hike out. A very strong party can do it as a single very long day trip from the trailhead (~27 miles round trip, ~6,000 ft gain).
routes.bail: The route has no fixed rappel stations; retreat at any point is by reversing the ascent line down the southwest slopes to Indian Pass and out the North Fork Sauk Trail. The most exposed section is the short corkscrew move onto the southeast face near the true summit -- parties uncomfortable with that Class 3 move can safely stop at the west (false) summit.
routes.turnaround: Given the length of the approach (roughly 11 miles and 4,000+ ft to Indian Pass before any climbing begins), set a firm turnaround time based on your slowest party member's pace and remaining daylight, and account for the off-trail, routefinding-dependent nature of the southwest slopes and the loose, exposed final scramble to the true summit.
routes.bestSeason: July through September offers the most favorable weather and driest conditions for this off-trail scramble; snow can linger on the upper slopes into early summer, and the long approach through the Glacier Peak Wilderness is best done once the high country has substantially melted out.
routes.comms: This is a remote, deep-backcountry route within the Glacier Peak Wilderness; cell coverage is generally unreliable to absent through the North Fork Sauk valley and at Indian Pass, consistent with the general lack of cell service reported across the WA Cascades backcountry away from highway corridors.
routes.face: South/Southwest Face to West Ridge to Southeast Face (final summit block)
routes.fa: Peak first climbed July 2, 1870 by railroad survey party members D.C. Linsley and John A. Tennant (this southwest-slopes/west-ridge line is the peak's natural easiest route and presumed line of that ascent; no distinct route-specific first-ascent record exists).

blurb: Mount Lago (8,745 ft) is the highest peak in the Okanogan Range and the second-highest summit in the Pasayten Wilderness after Jack Mountain, rising east of the Cascade crest in Okanogan County, Washington, within the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest. It is named for Lage Wernstedt (1878-1959), a Forest Service surveyor who mapped and first-ascended many North Cascades peaks in the 1920s ("Lago" being a play on Americans' pronunciation of "Lage"). The mountain is a complex, crumbly peak marked by pink porphyry intrusions, slabby sections, rocky ribs, and headwalls on its east flank, and is climbed almost exclusively as a remote, multi-day scramble rather than a technical rock objective.

routes.fa: Hermann Ulrichs and Dick Alt, 1933 (peak first ascent, likely via this line - see corrections)
objHaz: swollen creek crossing on approach (Eureka Creek, snowmelt season)
afternoon thunderstorms on exposed talus and ridges
grizzly and black bear country near the Canadian border
long remote approach with no quick bailout
routes.face: South Face / South Slope (ascent) - Southwest Slope (descent)
routes.overview: The standard route climbs Mount Lago's loose south side: an unroped Class 2-3 scramble up talus, scree, and rocky ribs from a high camp at Shellrock Basin, typically descended via the adjacent Southwest Slope.
routes.beta: Mount Lago has no maintained summit trail and sees relatively few visitors given its remote, multi-day approach deep in the Pasayten Wilderness. Nearly all parties climb it from a backcountry camp at Fred's Lake or Shellrock Basin, ascending the South Slope-South Face - a straightforward but tedious scramble over notoriously loose rock - often combined with neighboring Mount Carru via the connecting saddle, or with Osceola Peak and Mount Rolo as part of a longer Pasayten peak-bagging trip or ridge traverse.
routes.approach: From the Robinson Creek Trailhead (~2,500 ft, via SR-20 and Lost River Road/Forest Road 5400 west of Winthrop) or the higher Slate Peak Trailhead (~7,170 ft, via the Hart's Pass spur road), follow the Robinson Creek/Middle Fork Pasayten Trail over Robinson Pass and down into the Middle Fork Pasayten drainage. Continue north past the Eureka Creek Trail junction (~5,190 ft) to Fred's Lake (6,507 ft), a common camp and water source below Lago's west side. From Fred's Lake, follow the unmaintained middle section of the Eureka Creek Trail (or cross-country) east across the upper Eureka Creek basin - the creek crossing can be difficult when swollen with snowmelt - to reach Shellrock Basin/Shellrock Pass on the south side of the Lago-Carru ridge. The shortest road-based approach is roughly 16 miles one-way, typically covered over 1-2 days.
routes.descent: Reverse the South Slope, or descend via the adjacent Southwest Slope talus back to Shellrock Basin.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the loose South Slope/South Face talus and scree, or drop via the Southwest Slope variant, which avoids some of the steeper upper rock ribs. Many parties traverse instead to the Lago-Carru col to tag Mount Carru before returning to camp rather than descending fully and re-climbing. The rock is loose throughout, so move carefully, stay spread out, and watch for parties below.
routes.itinerary: A common 3-day plan: Day 1 - hike in from the Robinson Creek or Slate Peak trailhead to Fred's Lake or Shellrock Basin. Day 2 - climb Mount Lago (often combined with Mount Carru via the connecting saddle) from a Shellrock Basin camp, roughly a half-day round trip. Day 3 - hike out. Many parties extend to 4-5 days to add Osceola Peak, Mount Rolo, or a full Pasayten ridge traverse (e.g. via Ptarmigan Peak and Dot Mountain).
routes.bail: Retreat on the South Slope/South Face itself is straightforward - simply reverse your ascent/descent line on the talus. The greater commitment is the long, remote approach: once deep in the Middle Fork Pasayten/Eureka Creek drainage, the nearest trailhead is a full day's walk or more away, so any bail from Shellrock Basin still means a long trip out.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting the summit scramble so the loose descent and any creek crossings are completed in daylight; afternoon thunderstorms are common in the North Cascades/Okanogan Highlands in summer, and the open talus and ridges offer no shelter from lightning.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September is the reliable window, after the approach trail and Eureka Creek crossing have melted out and before fall snow returns to the high country. The trailheads themselves (especially the higher Slate Peak access) are typically only open roughly July-September/October depending on snowpack, and early-season attempts can face a difficult, swollen Eureka Creek ford.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable to nonexistent through most of the approach and on the peak itself; reliable coverage generally resumes only near Winthrop and along SR-20. Given the remote, multi-day nature of the trip, carrying a satellite messenger (e.g. Garmin inReach) is strongly advisable.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rack is needed - the standard South Slope/South Face line is an unroped Class 2-3 scramble over talus, scree, and rocky ribs. An ice axe (and possibly light traction) is worth carrying into early summer for lingering snow on the approach and summit slopes.

Map and compass or GPS (unmaintained trail sections)

proTips: Camp at Fred's Lake and/or Shellrock Basin to stage for Lago, Carru, and Osceola together on one multi-day trip.
Combine with Mount Carru via the connecting Lago-Carru saddle for an efficient two-peak day from a shared camp.
Fred's Lake is a reliable, scenic water and camp stop roughly midway along the approach.
watchOut: Rockfall risk is elevated on the loose south-facing slopes, especially with multiple parties on the mountain at once.
The middle section of the Eureka Creek Trail is unmaintained/abandoned - budget extra time and bring solid navigation skills.
Eureka Creek can be a difficult, slick-log or wade crossing during peak snowmelt (June-early July).
knownHazards: The upper mountain is composed of notoriously loose, crumbly rock requiring careful, deliberate movement throughout the ascent.
This is grizzly and black bear country near the Canadian border; proper food storage is essential.
Long stretches of open talus and ridge above treeline offer no protection from sudden thunderstorms.

climate.typical: Dry, sunny summers east of the Cascade crest with real afternoon thunderstorm potential; drier overall than the west-side Cascades but still fully alpine and subject to sudden weather.
climate.spring: Deep snowpack typically lingers into June-July at these elevations; trailheads and passes are often inaccessible.
climate.summer: The primary climbing season (Jul-Sep); warm days, cool nights, with a real risk of afternoon thunderstorms.
climate.fall: Can bring stable, cool, dry conditions into September/early October, but snow can return abruptly and end the season.
climate.winter: Road and trail access is effectively closed; deep snow and avalanche terrain.

routes.corrections: Multiple reputable sources (Wikipedia, PeakVisor, and the Peakbagger-sourced elevation cited in Cascade Climbers trip-report titles) consistently give Mount Lago's elevation as 8,745 ft, not 8,748 ft as in the provided peak data; 8,745 ft was used for this route's highPointFt. Also note a minor first-ascent discrepancy: Wikipedia's infobox credits the peak's first ascent to Hermann Ulrichs and Dick Alt in 1933, while a SummitPost community note speculates that Lage Wernstedt (the mountain's namesake surveyor) may have made an earlier, unconfirmed ascent around 1926 during his 1920s mapping campaign in the area; the better-documented 1933 Ulrichs/Alt ascent was used here, but this should be treated as the peak's first ascent (not confirmed as specific to this route).

blurb: Remmel Mountain (8,685 ft) is the highest peak in the Cathedral Range, a subrange of the Okanogan Range within the North Cascades, and the most prominent summit in the Pasayten Wilderness (Okanogan County, WA), rising about 5 miles south of the Canadian border. Built of granite, it climbs roughly 2,300 feet above Andrews Creek in under a mile. It was named in 1898 by pioneer surveyor Albert H. Sylvester and first climbed July 26, 1904 by Sledge Tatum and George E. Louden Jr. of the U.S.-Canada Boundary Survey party; a Forest Service fire lookout stood on its summit from the 1930s until it was removed/destroyed by 1969 after repeated lightning strikes.

routes.overview: The standard route follows a well-established unofficial climbers' trail up Remmel's southeast slope from Four Point Lake — a long, remote but non-technical Class 1-2 talus-and-meadow walk-up to the summit.
routes.beta: Remmel is a classic Pasayten peak-bagging objective: a big, remote walk-up rather than a technical climb. Most parties backpack in via one of three trailheads (Thirtymile/Chewuch River Trail to Four Point Lake, the shorter but rougher Andrews Creek approach, or the Cathedral Driveway) and then follow an obvious boot path up open talus and meadow slopes to the broad summit.
routes.approach: From the Thirtymile Trailhead (end of FR 5160, ~3,400 ft) or the Andrews Creek Trailhead (end of FR 51, ~3,050 ft), hike into the Pasayten Wilderness. Via Thirtymile: follow the Chewuch River Trail and Four Point Creek Trail to Four Point Lake (6,850 ft), then pick up the abandoned Coleman Ridge Trail; at roughly 7,070 ft a cairn marks the point to leave the trail and climb open meadow/talus up-valley to the summit on a well-trodden unofficial path. Via Andrews Creek: hike the Andrews Creek Trail roughly 9-11 miles (crossing heavy deadfall in an old 2003 burn in the lower miles) to a low saddle between Remmel and Coleman Ridge, then contour the west side of Coleman Ridge to reach the same upper slopes; alternatively continue to Andrews Pass (~6,700 ft) and climb one of several Class 3 west-facing gullies directly to the summit ridge.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route on foot back to Four Point Lake/Andrews Pass and out the inbound trailhead.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the talus/meadow boot path back to the Coleman Ridge Trail junction near 7,070 ft, then follow the trail back to Four Point Lake (or, via the Andrews Creek line, back down the Class 3 gullies or around via the Coleman Ridge contour) and out the approach trail to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 2-3 day backpack: Day 1 hike in to camp at Four Point Lake or along the Chewuch/Andrews Creek Trail; Day 2 climb to the summit and return to camp; Day 3 hike out. Strong parties have also completed the whole round trip (25+ miles, 5,500-6,800 ft gain) as a single very long day.
routes.bail: There are no technical sections to protect on the standard line — bail simply means turning around and retracing the boot path/trail to camp. On the Andrews Pass gully variant, downclimb the gully or shift to an adjacent one if it proves too loose or exposed.
routes.turnaround: Given the remoteness (16+ miles one-way) and open, exposed summit slopes, set a firm turnaround time that leaves ample daylight margin for the long return hike, and retreat early if afternoon thunderstorms build over the exposed upper mountain.
routes.bestSeason: Trail access typically opens around July 1 as snow clears the east-side approaches, and the route stays in condition until the first autumn snowfall (commonly October); the most reliable window is mid-July through September.
routes.comms: No verified cell coverage; this is deep wilderness terrain well over 15 miles from any road — carry a satellite messenger/PLB.

climate.forecastZone: East-slope North Cascades / Pasayten Wilderness (rain-shadow, continental-influenced)
climate.typical: Dry, sunny summers with occasional afternoon thunderstorms; noticeably drier and warmer than the west-side Cascades due to the rain shadow.
climate.spring: Snow lingers into June on the approach trails; higher terrain often not clear until late June/July.
climate.summer: Warm, dry days with cool nights; afternoon thunderstorm risk on exposed ridges and the summit.
climate.fall: Clear, cool, and stable through September, with the first snowfall of the season typically closing out access in October.
climate.winter: Cold, snowbound, and largely inaccessible; snow falls October-May across the wilderness.

routes.overview: A rarely-climbed alpine ridge line up Remmel's long ridge north of the summit above Remmel Lake, combining sections of 3rd-class scrambling with two short 5.1-5.2 pitches over gendarmes.
routes.beta: From a high camp at roughly 7,500 ft on the lower ridge, the route follows a long, stable, lightly-vegetated boulder ridge north of the summit. Two main gendarmes block easy passage; each is climbed at about 5.1-5.2 and then a rappel on the west side regains the ridge crest. The Beckey guide reportedly notes the line 'appears sensible as a route'; it sees very little traffic. Parties can bypass the technical sections by staying lower on the west slope, avoiding the vertical sections but missing the ridge's technical interest.
routes.approach: From Remmel Lake, bushwhack roughly 2 miles up the forested slope due south of the lake, through mosquito-infested timber and subalpine bog, to a high camp at treeline (~7,500 ft) on the lower NW ridge.
routes.descent: Reverse the ridge, downclimbing the 3rd-class sections and rappelling the two gendarmes back to high camp.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the upper ridge scrambling to the first gendarme, rappel or downclimb it, continue down the ridge to the second gendarme, rappel it, and continue 3rd-class scrambling back to the high camp at ~7,500 ft; from there retrace the bushwhack to Remmel Lake.
routes.bail: Retreat is off the ridge itself — downclimb or rappel back the way you came; there is no faster alternative descent given the remote, off-trail approach.
routes.turnaround: Because both the approach and descent require a slow, off-trail bushwhack and the technical sections need rappels, set a turnaround time with several hours of daylight buffer for the descent and return bushwhack.
routes.comms: No cell coverage in this remote, off-trail section of the Pasayten backcountry; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.fa: August 10, 2011 (ascending party not identified in the source record)
routes.rappels: 2 rappels (west side of the ridge) to regain the crest past the two main gendarmes
routes.face: NW Ridge (north side of the peak, above Remmel Lake)
routes.detailedRack: Small rack of nuts (or a light cam set) plus extra long slings for building rappel anchors on the gendarmes; a single 50-60m rope handles both raps.
routes.proNeeds: A light trad rack (nuts/small cams) suffices for the two roped pitches; bring extra slings/cord for rap anchors since fixed anchors are unlikely on this rarely-climbed line.
gear: Extra long slings/cord for rap anchors
Approach shoes or light boots for the bushwhack
whatToBring: Bivy/camping gear for a high camp at ~7,500 ft
Bug protection (approach is mosquito-infested)
Extra water/filter
Navigation tools for the trailless approach
proTips: Camp on the ridge itself at ~7,500 ft rather than lower, for better rock underfoot and less bushwhacking to start climbing.
It's possible to avoid the roped sections by staying lower on the west slope, but this bypasses the route's two technical gendarmes.
The lower ridge offers wild, scenic camp spots on stable, lightly-vegetated granite blocks.
watchOut: Sustained, difficult bushwhacking to reach the ridge
Loose rock on the gendarmes
Very little traffic/beta — expect route-finding uncertainty
Rappel anchors may need to be built from scratch
knownHazards: Remote, seldom-visited technical terrain far from help
Off-trail approach through boggy, mosquito-heavy forest
Exposed rappels on the west side of the gendarmes

pitchDetail.notes: Ridge scrambling from high camp (~7,500 ft) on stable, lightly vegetated boulders.

Rappel the west side of the ridge (single 50-60m rope) to regain the crest past the gendarme.
Climb the second main gendarme.
Second rappel on the west side to regain the ridge.
Continue ridge scrambling to the summit.

blurb: Robinson Mountain (8,726–8,731 ft depending on source) is a broad, remote summit anchoring the southern edge of the Pasayten Wilderness in the Okanogan Range, a sub-range of the North Cascades in western Okanogan County, Washington, on land administered by the Okanogan–Wenatchee National Forest. It ranks among Washington's "Bulger" list of 100 highest peaks and was first climbed in 1904 by Sledge Tatum and George E. Louden Jr.; the mountain is named for settler brothers James and Thomas Robinson. The peak is composed of loose, broken talus and scree typical of the range, and its standard line is a long approach followed by a class 2–3 ridge scramble rather than a technical rock route.

routes.overview: The standard and easiest line on Robinson Mountain: a long trail approach up Robinson Creek and unmaintained Beauty Creek Trail to a tarn basin, then a scree climb to a gap on the southeast ridge followed by roughly a mile of class 2 ridge walking with a few class 3 sections — including a short, exposed knife-edge/gully crux — to the broad summit.
routes.beta: From the Robinson Creek Trailhead, follow the maintained Robinson Creek Trail south/southeast, cross Beauty Creek, and turn uphill onto the unmarked, unmaintained Beauty Creek Trail. Climb the brushy, steep Beauty Creek valley, cross a tributary around 5,200-5,260 ft, then ascend open meadow/scree to a small tarn at roughly 6,750-6,800 ft in a rocky basin. From the tarn, climb the loose scree slope (snow-covered in early season) north of the tarn to a gap in the southeast ridge, then turn left (south) and follow the ridge about a mile over several false summits — mostly class 2 walking with a few class 3 sections and a narrow, exposed crux just below the true summit.
routes.approach: From Winthrop, drive Highway 20 west about 14-15 miles to the Mazama junction, then either (a) continue on Lost River Road (County Road 9140) about 8.75 miles (paved then gravel Forest Road 5400) to Robinson Creek Trailhead, or (b) turn onto the Harts Pass Road (also FR-5400) and drive about 9.0 miles to the same trailhead (elev. ~2,500-2,560 ft, with an information sign, picnic table, and vault toilet). From the trailhead, hike the Robinson Creek - Middle Fork Trail (#478) south for about 2.8 miles to the signed/bridged Beauty Creek junction (elev. ~3,560 ft) just past a bridge over Beauty Creek. Turn right (northeast/uphill) onto the unmarked, overgrown Beauty Creek Trail and follow it about 1.5 miles up the valley, staying northwest of the creek, to a tributary crossing around 5,200-5,260 ft, then continue up open slopes to the tarn basin at about 6,750-6,800 ft.
routes.descent: Descend the same southeast ridge and scree slope back to the tarn, then retrace the Beauty Creek and Robinson Creek trails to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, reverse the ridge scramble north along the crest back to the gap, downclimb the scree slope to the tarn basin, then follow the boot path/open slopes back down to the Beauty Creek tributary crossing (~5,200 ft) and down the steep, brushy Beauty Creek Trail to its junction with the Robinson Creek Trail. From there it is a straightforward 2.8-mile walk out the maintained Robinson Creek Trail to the trailhead. The loose scree section above and below the tarn is the main slow/careful-footing section on both ascent and descent.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as a very long single day (~11-14 miles round trip, ~6,100-6,500 ft gain, reported car-to-car times around 12 hours) or as an overnight, camping at the Southeast Robinson Tarn (or lower in Beauty Creek valley or at the Robinson Creek bridge) to split the approach and summit push into two days.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward along the ascent line: the only exposed, non-trivial section is the short class 3 knife-edge/gully move just below the summit, which can be downclimbed carefully or the attempt abandoned before committing to it; below the ridge gap, the scree slope and Beauty Creek valley offer an uncomplicated walk-out back to the Robinson Creek Trail.
routes.turnaround: Given the long approach (11+ miles round trip with 6,000+ ft of gain) and a full day of exposure to sun and afternoon thunderstorm risk on an open ridge with no shelter, set a firm turnaround time based on daylight and current weather/energy rather than pushing to complete the ridge traverse late in the day.
routes.bestSeason: Typically climbed June through September/early October, after the access road has opened and snow has cleared from the upper scree slopes and tarn basin. Trip reports describe good snow-climbing conditions in the summit cirque and ridge approach around Memorial Day (late May), while mid/late-summer ascents (July) are snow-free but hot, arid, and exposed with minimal shade and water only reliably available at the tarn and creek crossings.
objHaz: extreme sun exposure with little shade
limited water beyond creek crossings and tarn
routes.proNeeds: No technical protection required; this is an unroped class 2-3 scramble on talus/scree and broken ridge crest.
gear: helmet (recommended for the loose scree and summit crux)
ice axe (early-season snow in the cirque/chute)
whatToBring: large volume of water (little to none available above the tarn)
sun protection (hat, sunscreen, sunglasses - route is largely shadeless)
map/compass or GPS (route-finding experience required on unmarked Beauty Creek Trail and scree)
wilderness self-issue permit (fill out at trailhead)
proTips: The Beauty Creek Trail turnoff just past the Beauty Creek bridge is unsigned and overgrown with brush — watch closely for the boot path rather than continuing on the main Robinson Creek Trail.
Camping at the Southeast Robinson Tarn (~6,750-6,800 ft) splits a very long day into a manageable two-day trip and provides the only reliable high water source.
Carry more water than feels necessary; multiple trip reports describe the scree slopes and ridge as hot, dry, and shadeless in summer.
watchOut: The Beauty Creek Trail junction is easy to miss; it is unmarked and hidden in brush just past the Beauty Creek bridge.
The scree slope below the ridge gap is loose and can be snow-covered and slick in early season.
The final class 3 section just below the summit is narrow and exposed on both sides — take care with route-finding and footing.
knownHazards: Rattlesnakes have been reported on the lower approach trail.
Creek crossings (Robinson Creek, Beauty Creek, and the West Fork Beauty Creek tributary) can run high during spring snowmelt.
Breakable snow crust over downed logs in the forested lower approach has been reported in late-May conditions.

pitchDetail.notes: 2.8 mi on maintained Robinson Creek - Middle Fork Trail #478 from trailhead (~2,560 ft) to the Beauty Creek junction (~3,560 ft).
~1.5 mi up the brushy, steep, unmarked Beauty Creek Trail to a tributary crossing around 5,200-5,260 ft.
Open meadow and loose scree/talus gaining roughly 1,500-1,600 ft to the tarn basin at ~6,750-6,800 ft; can be snow-covered in early season.
Ascend the loose scree slope north of the tarn to a gap in the southeast ridge.
About 1 mile of ridge crest south to the summit, over several false summits; mostly class 2 walking.
Short, narrow, exposed knife-edge/gully step just below the true summit; a helmet is recommended for loose rock.

climate.typical: East-of-the-crest Cascades climate: sunnier and drier than the western Cascades, with hot, exposed summer days on the upper ridge and scree.
climate.spring: Snow lingers in the tarn cirque and summit chute into late May/June; creek crossings run high with snowmelt.
climate.summer: Hot, dry, and largely shadeless on the approach and ridge; carry ample water and watch for afternoon thunderstorms.
climate.fall: Generally stable and dry into September/early October before winter snow returns.
climate.winter: Heavy snow closes the access road; not a winter objective.
