hierarchyNote: Identity confirmed, not a mismatch — flagging only a minor elevation-source discrepancy: the given coordinates (48.694408, -121.042952) match Wikipedia's cited coordinates for Ruby Mountain, WA (48°41'40"N 121°02'34"W) almost exactly, and the given elevation of 7,426 ft matches listsofjohn.com's listing. However, Wikipedia/USGS-derived figures elsewhere cite 7,407-7,412 ft (NAVD88) for the same summit — almost certainly a different survey/datum rather than a different peak, since coordinates and all other identifying data (Ross Lake NRA, above Diablo Lake, SR 20, Colonial Peak as nearest higher neighbor) agree across every source. Parent area "North Cascades Core" is geographically correct: Ruby Mountain sits within Ross Lake National Recreation Area / North Cascades National Park Complex, directly above Diablo Lake along the North Cascades Highway. No changes made to the given elevation since it is independently well-sourced.
blurb: Ruby Mountain (7,426 ft) is a prominent summit on the west side of Ross Lake National Recreation Area in Washington's North Cascades, rising directly above Diablo Lake and the North Cascades Highway (SR 20) near Colonial Creek Campground. Built of Eocene-to-Cretaceous schist and carrying 3,888 feet of clean topographic prominence (21st-most prominent peak in Washington), it offers panoramic views of Colonial Peak, Snowfield Peak's Neve Glacier, Ross Lake, Hozomeen Mountain, and Jack Mountain. First climbed in 1916 by U.S. Forest Service ranger Tommy Thompson, it is popular today as a strenuous, non-technical scramble via its south, northwest, or (in winter/spring) Happy Creek approaches.

routes.overview: The standard summer route: a long approach up the Thunder Creek Trail to Fourth of July Pass, followed by an abandoned bootpath and open scree/heather slopes up Ruby's south ridge to the summit.
routes.beta: From Colonial Creek Campground, follow the popular Thunder Creek Trail and then Fourth of July Pass Trail to the broad, forested pass at ~3,600 ft. At the pass, an unsigned, unmaintained path (the old 'Ruby Mountain Way Trail') marked by a jagged 8-ft stump and orange flagging on the north side of the main trail climbs through deadfall and heather benches onto the open south ridge, finishing on wide-open scree and talus to the summit. Non-technical but long, with real route-finding difficulty in the mid-mountain deadfall zone.
routes.approach: From Marblemount, drive SR 20 east about 24 miles; just past milepost 130, before crossing the Thunder Arm of Diablo Lake, turn into Colonial Creek Campground and proceed to the Thunder Creek Trailhead at the back of the campground near the boat ramp. Hike the Thunder Creek Trail roughly 1.7-2.1 miles to the Fourth of July Pass Trail junction, then continue steeply up the west slopes about 5.2 miles total (2,629 ft gain) to Fourth of July Pass.
routes.descent: Descend the same south-ridge route back to Fourth of July Pass and out the Thunder Creek Trail.
routes.descentText: Retrace the ascent: down the open scree/talus and heather benches of the south ridge, back through the deadfall zone (watch for the cairn/flagging near 4,950 ft) to the old trail's start at Fourth of July Pass, then down the maintained Thunder Creek Trail to the campground trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Trailhead to Fourth of July Camp (4.8 mi, ~2,300 ft gain, ~2.7 hrs), set up camp (permanent water source at camp, but none higher on the mountain by mid-summer). Day 2: Camp to summit (~4,000 ft gain, ~6.1 hrs), summit to camp (~3.6 hrs), camp to trailhead (~1.7 hrs). Strong parties can also do it as a single very long day.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward on this out-and-back line — descend back to Fourth of July Pass and the maintained Thunder Creek Trail from anywhere on the south ridge; there is no technical descent or rappelling required.
routes.turnaround: Given the roughly 17-mile round trip and 6,200+ ft of gain, with an unmaintained, hard-to-follow trail above Fourth of July Pass, set a firm turnaround time before starting that guarantees clearing the deadfall/route-finding section and the open upper ridge with daylight and energy to spare for the return.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-July through September is most reliable, once the upper mountain sheds snow (WTA notes a snow-free climb is 'normally possible halfway through July'). Earlier-season ascents are possible on firm spring snow with an ice axe, per SummitPost trip reports.
whatToBring: Water treatment (sources dry up above Fourth of July Camp by mid-summer)
Extra layers for the exposed open ridge
Sun protection
Bug repellent (early summer)
Headlamp for an early start/late return
proTips: Start very early given the ~17-mile round trip and 6,200+ ft of gain.
At Fourth of July Pass, look for a jagged 8-ft stump with orange flagging on the north side of the main trail — it marks the start of the old Ruby Mountain Way Trail.
Fill water at Fourth of July Camp; sources above it are typically dry by mid-summer.
The 3,900-4,900 ft deadfall zone is the trickiest section for route-finding — watch for cairns and flagging.
watchOut: Abandoned, unmaintained trail with deadfall and lost tread between roughly 3,900 and 4,900 ft
Steep heather slopes above 5,800 ft can be slick when wet
Early-season snow can obscure the route and require an ice axe
Very long day (~17 mi, 6,200+ ft) — many parties split it over two days via Fourth of July Camp
knownHazards: Significant route-finding difficulty on the unmaintained upper trail
Deadfall/blowdown sections mid-mountain
Loose talus/scree near the summit
Afternoon thunderstorm exposure on the open upper ridge

routes.overview: A steep, direct hike/scramble straight up Ruby's northwest ridge from a Highway 20 turnout — the most popular way up during winter and spring, when SR 20 is gated for the season.
routes.beta: Park at a turnout across from a large boulder field on the northwest side of the mountain. Climb steeply up the hillside left (east) of the boulder field to gain the northwest ridge, follow it over Point 6315', transition onto the west ridge around 5,400 ft, and finish with a scramble up the summit ridge. In snow season, cliff bands along the ridge are typically turned via snow ramps.
routes.approach: From Newhalem, drive or walk SR 20 east about 12 miles (0.4 mi west of Horsetail Creek, ~2.8 mi northeast of Colonial Creek Campground) to a turnout at about 1,900 ft elevation, directly across from a large boulder field on Ruby's northwest side.
routes.descent: Descend the same northwest/west ridge line back to the highway turnout.
routes.descentText: Retrace the ascent ridge, using snow ramps to negotiate cliff bands in winter/spring conditions; remove snowshoes for one cliffy section around 4,000 ft as needed on the way down as well.
routes.bail: The ridge can be descended from essentially any point back to the highway; no technical downclimbing or rappels are required outside of unusually icy conditions.
routes.turnaround: Snow conditions on this open, partly north/west-facing ridge deteriorate through the day (crust breaking into cold powder, or wet-slab risk on warm-ups); set a turnaround time that gets you off the open ridge before afternoon warming or worsening weather.
routes.bestSeason: Winter through spring (roughly December-May) while SR 20 is closed to vehicles beyond the gate — this is the standard cold-season line up Ruby. Compact spring snow (April-May) is often cited as easier and lower-avalanche-risk than deep winter snowpack.
whatToBring: Avalanche gear (beacon, probe, shovel) for winter ascents
Extra insulating layers for the exposed open ridge
Sunglasses/sun protection for snow glare
Headlamp
proTips: This is the most popular way up when SR 20 is gated for winter — check current WSDOT plow status, since the drivable/walkable distance to the turnout changes through the season.
Cliff bands on the ridge are usually turned via snow ramps early season; reassess them individually as the snowpack changes.
Snowshoes are commonly removed through one cliffy section around 4,000 ft and put back on above it.
watchOut: The base boulder field can be hazardous to cross with only partial snow cover
Cliff bands along the ridge require careful, conditions-dependent route-finding
Spring crust can break through into cold, faceted powder underneath
Wet-slab avalanche danger on north/west aspects during warm-ups
knownHazards: Avalanche terrain on the upper open ridge and cliff-band snow ramps
Cliff bands requiring exposed route-finding
Winter highway closure adds mileage/logistics depending on plow status

routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable in this remote stretch of the North Cascades; do not rely on a phone alone for winter rescue.

routes.overview: A winter/spring line up Ruby's north side following the Happy Creek drainage — described by regional sources as the least demanding way up the mountain in the right snow conditions.
routes.beta: From the Happy Creek Nature Trail area on SR 20, leave the road just east of Happy Creek and climb its east side through forest, following (or starting on) the Happy Creek Nature Trail about 1.5 miles to a waterfall. Above the falls the bootpath steepens and grows fainter but stays followable, crossing a boulder field (snow-covered in the right season) before continuing up the north-side slopes to the summit.
routes.approach: In deep winter, park at the SR 20 closure gate (typically near the Ross Dam Trailhead) and walk/ski the closed highway to the Happy Creek area. In spring, when WSDOT has plowed several additional miles toward Happy Creek, park at the Happy Creek Nature Trail lot directly.
routes.descent: Descend the same Happy Creek drainage line back to the highway.
routes.descentText: Retrace the ascent down the north-side slopes and through the boulder field, back down the (now fainter) bootpath through forest, rejoining the Happy Creek Nature Trail near the waterfall for the final descent to the road.
routes.bail: As an out-and-back line with no technical terrain, retreat is possible from any point by descending back into the Happy Creek drainage toward the highway.
routes.turnaround: This is a winter/spring avalanche-terrain route on the mountain's north side; set a turnaround time that keeps you off open slopes before daily warming or precipitation increases avalanche danger, and allow extra time if approaching from the winter highway closure gate rather than a plowed trailhead.
routes.bestSeason: Winter through spring (roughly December-April), and specifically noted as easiest once WSDOT has plowed extra miles of SR 20 toward Happy Creek in early spring, shortening the approach.
objHaz: Hidden boulder field under thin snow
whatToBring: Extra traction for firm/icy morning snow
Sun protection for spring snow travel
Map/GPS — the upper bootpath is faint and route-finding depends partly on established tracks
proTips: Regional trip reports describe this as the least demanding way up Ruby Mountain in the right winter/spring conditions.
Wait for WSDOT to plow a few extra miles of SR 20 toward Happy Creek in spring — it substantially shortens the approach versus starting from the main winter gate.
Established skin/boot tracks from other parties make the upper open terrain considerably easier to navigate.
watchOut: The boulder field below treeline is hazardous — risk of punching through and twisting an ankle — without adequate snow cover
The skin track/bootpath has been reported steep, frozen, and low-traction in cold conditions
Avalanche potential on open upper terrain during the Cascades' wet, heavy snow events
knownHazards: Avalanche terrain in the Happy Creek drainage and open upper slopes
Hidden boulder-field hazard under thin/patchy snow
Highway winter-gate logistics add distance depending on plow status

pitchDetail.pitch: Highway/closure gate to Happy Creek Nature Trail split
pitchDetail.notes: Roughly 10 minutes along the highway before leaving it just east of Happy Creek.

pitchDetail.pitch: Happy Creek Nature Trail to waterfall (~1.5 mi)
pitchDetail.notes: Follows the well-built nature trail up the east side of Happy Creek to a cascading waterfall.
Path steepens and grows fainter but stays followable; crosses a boulder field that is hazardous without snow cover.
Snow cover typically begins near 2,800 ft; continue up the north-side terrain to the summit.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR 20)
road.status: Paved; approach depends on winter plow status
road.seasonalGate: SR 20's main winter closure gate is typically near the Ross Dam Trailhead; WSDOT sometimes plows several additional miles toward Happy Creek in early spring, which shortens this approach considerably.
road.driveNote: Park at the current winter closure gate or, once plowed, at the Happy Creek Nature Trail lot on SR 20 east of Newhalem.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate; heavy Cascade snowpack on this north-facing drainage in winter.
climate.spring: Snow gradually consolidates; regional trip reports note this as prime timing.
climate.winter: SR 20 gated near Ross Dam; high avalanche danger with wet, heavy Cascade snow.

access.landManager: National Park Service — Ross Lake National Recreation Area, North Cascades National Park Complex
access.fees: No day-use fee for a day climb.
access.permit: No permit needed for a day climb; overnight camping in the park requires a backcountry permit.
access.closures: Approach length depends entirely on the current SR 20 winter plow line; check WSDOT road status before a trip.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning to take advantage of firmer overnight-refrozen snow and to be off open slopes before afternoon warming
